verbatim_text: So, now let's take a long sequence of words given at test time. So, this is a test sequence. And let me just take the sliding window transformer that had constant latency, but worse loss. And instead of only doing a forward pass on the sliding window transformer, as it processes the first window of tokens, I'm going to do both a forward pass and a backward pass. And now, as it moves on to the second window of words, the first window of words has been stored in the weights of this model because of the backward pass. And as you move on to the second window, you do the same thing, and then you move on to the third, and so on and so forth. And as you have gone through this entire sequence, you have done a lot of forward and backward passes, each on a limited window of words. And the hope is that by the time you are here, even though you're only attending to this little chunk of words, all of these words before have been stored in your weights already. Now, compare this to a transformer with sliding windows, sorry, with self-attention, full attention over the entire context, that only does a giant forward pass. Two questions. First, I mean, you have all of these errors, all of these errors in the slide before. Is this really faster than just doing this one giant forward error? And the second is, are all of these errors before able to retain the information, as I said. So let's go back to the slide.
